name: Solmir
symbol: A pair of hands cradling a small flame
description: God of healing, companionship, and refuge. Solmir's followers believe care for others is the highest calling — not heroism, not power, but showing up for people who need it. Particular emphasis on refugees, the displaced, and those rebuilding after loss. Solmir cares about suffering.
worship_notes: Order of Solmir operates refuges and healing houses. Revivify in Ravenquay deeded to the Order. Clerics tend to be found wherever people are struggling rather than wherever power is concentrated.
notes: Clergy take vows of service/poverty (monetary rewards go to charity)

name: Ghorrakh
description: Not a god of war. A god of endurance and continuation — the specific quality of still being here when you weren't supposed to be. The god of surviving things that should have finished you. Ghorrakh doesn't promise victory. Ghorrakh promises that if you're still standing, that means something.
worship_notes: Common among orc-kin and half-orcs though not exclusively. Not celebrated loudly — Ghorrakh's followers tend to mark survival quietly, privately. The acknowledgment is internal.
notes: Distinct from typical orc war god traditions found elsewhere. Endurance over aggression.

name: Aethros
description: *Law shields the weak. Order guards the innocent. Justice illuminates the truth.* Law exists to protect the innocent and punish the guilty. Oaths and promises are sacred - breaking them is grave sin. Justice must be balanced with mercy, but never compromised. Authority comes with responsibility to those you protect. Evil must be opposed actively, not passively tolerated
worship_notes: Formal prayers and structured rituals. Oath-taking ceremonies (very serious, magically binding). Trials and judgments blessed by clergy. Exorcisms and banishment rituals (as Thane performed). Maintaining ancient wards and seals (like the giant chambers)

name: Torven
symbol: two roads crossing with a small hand encircled at the intersection

name: Veleth
symbol: two hands reaching toward each other without quite touching
description: *Love is not one thing. It is every thing.* The space between people is sacred. Desire is honest. Friendship is love's quietest and most enduring form. Physical connection is neither shameful nor trivial — it is one of the clearest languages available to living things. Veleth makes no distinction between romantic love, deep friendship, or physical affection — all are expressions of the same fundamental reaching toward another.
worship_notes: No formal liturgy. Prayers are conversational, often whispered. Ceremonies mark connections between people rather than obligations to the deity. Temples are warm, candlelit, open at all hours. Clergy are listeners first. No vows of celibacy — considered counterproductive. Followers are encouraged to love specifically and honestly rather than generally and safely.
notes: Veleth is present whether worshipped or not. The deity doesn't require acknowledgment — only that people reach toward each other honestly. Clergy of Veleth are often found where people are suffering from isolation, loss, or broken connection. The symbol — two hands almost touching — represents both the beauty and the ache of reaching.

name: Carix
symbol: a coin standing on its edge
description: *Nothing that stays the same stays true.* Carix is not evil and is not good — Carix is the stone thrown into still water. Comfort breeds blindness. Certainty breeds arrogance. The unexpected consequence is not punishment — it is information. What falls apart when disrupted was already falling apart. What survives was always worth keeping. Carix does not cause chaos for its own sake but understands that what appears chaotic from inside the disruption often reveals necessary truth from outside it.
worship_notes: No fixed rituals — rituals become habit and habit is antithema to Carix. Prayers are improvisational. Temples rarely look like temples. Clergy change their methods constantly by principle. Followers are expected to question comfortable assumptions, disrupt stagnant situations, and accept that their own disruptions will sometimes produce consequences they didn't intend. That's the point. Carix asks only that followers remain honest about what they're doing and why.
notes: Carix and Veleth are often found working adjacent to each other — disruption frequently reveals buried feeling, and honest connection frequently disrupts comfortable arrangements. Their clergy have a complicated relationship that mostly manifests as mutual exasperated respect. Carix is present in every moment someone does the unexpected thing for the right reason.

name: Kexifrext
symbol: a single root growing around a stone — neither breaking it nor stopped by it
description: *What grows does not hurry. What hurries does not grow.* Kexifrext is not distant — Kexifrext is simply operating on a timescale that makes most beings feel like mayflies. The forests of the fey realm are not Kexifrext's domain in the sense of territory. They are Kexifrext's body, memory, and language simultaneously. A command from Kexifrext may take decades to arrive and decades more to fully understand. This is not negligence. This is how something that has existed since before the continent had its current shape communicates with a world that moves too fast to listen properly.
worship_notes: No clergy in the conventional sense — Kexifrext does not require intermediaries, only patience. Followers learn to sit with incomplete instructions and trust that the full meaning will arrive in time. Prayers are not spoken but grown — an action taken, a thing planted, a path walked slowly. Enauweyn is among the few mortals Kexifrext communicates with directly, and even she receives commands that take years to fully resolve. Followers are expected to protect old growth, resist those who would accelerate or exploit natural processes, and exercise judgment in the long silences between guidance.
notes: Kexifrext is neither good nor evil in any way mortals find useful to categorize. The forest does not have opinions about individual creatures — it has opinions about patterns over centuries. Fey shenanigans continue largely unchecked under Kexifrext's sovereignty not from indifference but because on Kexifrext's timescale, most fey mischief resolves itself. When Kexifrext does intervene, it is slow, inexorable, and final in the way that a root eventually splits a stone.
